one_line_takeaway: A comprehensive taxonomy survey of Federated Foundation Models across training/aggregation, trustworthiness (poisoning/privacy/IP threats), incentives, and evaluation, arguing that FM scale breaks inherited FL techniques -- security-relevant as a landscape reference, but it proposes no new system, benchmark, or threat model of its own.
key_finding: There is currently no FL aggregation technique purpose-built for FedFM; practice has defaulted to vanilla FedAvg/FedSGD despite FM-specific challenges. More broadly, the survey argues FM scale breaks or strains inherited FL techniques across all four pillars: existing Byzantine-robust defenses are of questionable effectiveness at FM scale, Shapley-Value contribution evaluation is impractical due to high latency and the 'compositional gap' of FMs, and standard privacy defenses must be re-balanced against knowledge integrity. The authors position FedFM as a way to broaden data horizons, distribute compute, mitigate AI monopolies, and preserve privacy, while flagging trustworthiness as the central unmet need.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (not an experimental paper). Its own contribution is a systematic multi-tiered taxonomy of Federated Foundation Models (FedFM) -- the integration of Foundation Models with Federated Learning -- organized around four pillars: (1) training/aggregation, (2) trustworthiness, (3) incentivization, and (4) evaluation. The authors synthesize and categorize existing FedFM approaches, argue theirs is the most comprehensive coverage to date (Table II positions it against 7 prior position/review papers across 8 dimensions), and lay out open challenges plus future directions, notably the speculative use of quantum computing for FedFM training/inference/optimization/security. NOTE: the many concrete methods discussed (FedPEFT, SLoRA, FedCLIP, FedBERT, FedPrompt, FedOBD, FedIT, FwdLLM, etc.) are CITED prior work being organized by the taxonomy, not the authors' own systems.
threat_model: As a survey, the paper does not propose or evaluate a novel threat model; it CATALOGS the FedFM threat landscape within its 'Trustworthiness' pillar (Section V). The synthesized threats are: (1) Poisoning attacks -- untargeted attacks that disrupt training/prevent convergence, and targeted attacks that subtly manipulate model output, countered by Byzantine-robust aggregation (geometric outlier detection, top-performance selection, hybrid schemes) whose effectiveness the survey says is questionable at FM scale; (2) Privacy attacks -- membership inference (was a sample in training?) and data reconstruction (recreate original training data), which FM scale worsens, with differential privacy, confidence masking, and model compression cited as defenses; (3) Intellectual-property / model-theft threats, addressed via black-box fine-tuning and watermarking. The security framing is genuine and native to the paper (a full section), not imposed.
limitations: As a broad survey, depth is traded for breadth -- security topics (poisoning, membership inference, reconstruction, watermarking) are summarized at the level of trends and strategies rather than compared quantitatively, so no defense is empirically ranked. Claims such as 'existing Byzantine defenses are questionable in FedFM settings' and the compositional-gap argument are asserted from cited literature rather than demonstrated. The quantum-computing-for-FedFM direction is forward-looking/speculative. Cited coverage reflects up to ~2024 (arXiv v4, Sep 2024).

quantitative_results: Survey scope claim: compares itself against 7 prior FedFM works (2 position papers + 5 short reviews) across 8 coverage dimensions in Table II (taxonomy, efficiency, trustworthiness, incentivization, evaluation, framework, challenges, future directions).

Cited background (not authors' own results): GPT-2 = 1.3B parameters, ~2.6 GB footprint; GPT-3 = 175B parameters, ~350 GB in half-precision, trained on ~300B tokens; GPT-4 = over 1 trillion parameters.
Cited background: LLaMA training required 2,048 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for ~21 days; PaLM required TPUv3 for ~30 days.
Cited background: research suggests potential depletion of high-quality language data by 2026.

Cataloged FedFM methods (Table III, others' work) report their base aggregator, e.g., FedPrompt/FedBERT/FedCLIP/FedPEFT/FedPETuning/SLoRA/FedOBD/FedIT all built on FedAvg, FwdLLM on FedSGD; survey observes no aggregator is tailored to FMs, so vanilla FedAvg/FedSGD dominate.
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines (survey). Closest analog: Table II qualitative coverage comparison against 7 prior reviews/position papers (Chen et al., Zhuang et al., Yu et al., Kang et al., Herbert et al., Li et al. x2).
Table I qualitative comparison of FedFM vs. traditional (centralized) Foundation Models across 8 factors.
